reason: The paper explicitly computes "mean decrease in the Gini index" averaged over 2500 random-forest runs to rank all 3054 antibodies, then uses both the magnitude ("overall average antibody importance was around 0.018" vs. top value 0.36) and rank position (e.g., EBNA1_430 "ranked in the 1866th place," EBNA6_0070 "not among the top most important antibodies") to conclude, in the paper's own Conclusions section, that "the role of EBV on eventual ME/CFS-related autoimmunity should be reconsidered under the lens of danger theory" — i.e., ranking/magnitude directly drives the central molecular-mimicry conclusion. The only corroboration offered is (a) a Spearman correlation between the same Gini importance values and sequence-homology scores (which reuses the potentially biased magnitudes as its input rather than independently validating them) and (b) held-out test accuracy of the final 26-antibody classifier (90%), which per the stated rubric validates that the selected set predicts disease status but does not validate the internal ranking/magnitude comparisons used to argue individual candidate antibodies were unimportant. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance was used, so the ranking claim central to the mimicry conclusion is not adequately corroborated.
